request.question: Higher education in Ukraine has a long and rich history, the pioneering research of scholars working in the country’s higher education institutions and academies, such as which Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics?

title: AQAS
Higher education in New Brunswick
Higher Education Commission
Zhukovsky (film)
ANUIES
List of universities and colleges in Denmark
List of universities in Pakistan
Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky
Higher education in Ukraine
List of universities in Australia
sentences: The Agency for Quality Assurance through Accreditation of Study Programs (AQAS) is an independent non-profit organisation dedicated for the accreditation of higher education institutions in Germany.
It is supported by more than 80 member institutions, both higher education institutions (HEIs) as well as academic associations.
AQAS was founded in 2002 and is headquartered in Cologne, North Rhine-Westphalia, Germany.
To date it has accredited more than 3,500 degree programs at universities and universities of applied sciences, including numerous programs outside of Germany.
In January 2009, the German Accreditation Council granted permission to AQAS to accredit quality assurance systems of higher education institutions as well.
Higher education in New Brunswick (also referred to as postsecondary education) refers to education provided by higher education institutions in the Canadian province of New Brunswick.
Higher education has a rich history in New Brunswick.
The first English-language university in Canada was the University of New Brunswick.
Mount Allison University was the first in the British Empire to award a baccalaureate to a woman, Grace Annie Lockhart, B.Sc.
Education is the responsibility of the provinces in Canada and there is no federal ministry governing it.
A Higher Education Commission or Commission on Higher Education is a private or government agency charged with Higher Education.
Commissions responsibilities includes regulating Higher Education, post-secondary education institutions, Universities and other public and private higher education institutions.
The Commission is often headed by the Minister for Education or Commissioner.

Zhukovsky (Russian: Жуковский ) is a 1950 Soviet biopic directed by Vsevolod Pudovkin and Dmitri Vasilyev, based on the life of Russian scientist Nikolai Zhukovsky (1847–1921), founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics.
In 1950 Pudovkin received the Best Director award at the 5th Karlovy Vary International Film Festival for this film.
In 1951 Pudovkin, Shebalin, Golovnya, and Belokurov received the Stalin Prize.

The National Association of Universities and Higher Education Institutions (Spanish: "Asociación Nacional de Universidades e Instituciones de Educación Superior", ANUIES) is a non-governmental organization which includes 152 public and private higher education institutions in Mexico.

The association is involved in the development of programs, plans and national policies for higher education, as well as establishing agencies aimed at fostering the development of higher education in the country.
Higher education in Denmark is offered by a range of universities, university colleges, business academies and specialised institutions.

The national higher education system is in accordance with the Bologna process, with bachelor's degrees (first cycle, three years), master's degrees (second cycle, two years) and doctoral degrees (third cycle, three years).

The majority of higher education institutions are the responsibility of the Ministry of Higher Education and Science (Denmark), however, some higher education institutions within the arts are the responsibility of the Ministry of Culture.
Higher education in Pakistan is the systematic process of students continuing their education beyond secondary school, learned societies, and two-year colleges.
The governance of higher education is maintained under the Higher Education Commission (HEC) which oversees the financial funding, research outputs, and teaching quality in the country.
In Pakistan, the higher education system includes the public, private, military, and vocational universities, all accredited by the HEC.

Since independence, new universities have expanded throughout the country with support provided by the University Grants Commission (UGC), which had been an autonomous institution of recognizing universities until 2002 when it was preceded by the HEC.
Pakistan produces about 445,000 university graduates and 10,000 computer science graduates annually.
A number of institutions of higher learning are active in the country, but the HEC recognizes 183 institutions.
This article provides a comprehensive list of higher education institutions active in Pakistan.

Prof Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky (Russian: Никола́й Его́рович Жуко́вский ; January 17 [O.S. January 5] 1847  – March 17, 1921) was a Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics.
Whereas contemporary scientists scoffed at the idea of human flight, Zhukovsky was the first to undertake the study of airflow.
He is often called the Father of Russian Aviation.
Higher education in Ukraine has a long and rich history.
Its students, graduates and academics have long been known and appreciated worldwide.

The pioneering research of scholars working in the country’s higher education institutions and academies, such as Dmytro Mendeleyev, Mykola Zhukovsky, and Yeugeny Paton, are part of the universal history of scientific progress.
There are 43 universities in Australia: 40 public universities, two international universities, and one private university.

The Commonwealth Higher Education Support Act 2003 sets out three groups of Australian higher education providers: universities, other self-accrediting higher education institutions, and state and territory accredited higher education institutions.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Higher education in Ukraine has a long and rich history, the pioneering research of scholars working in the country’s higher education institutions and academies, such as which Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics?
hop1_docs: Higher education in Ukraine | Higher education in Ukraine has a long and rich history.  Its students, graduates and academics have long been known and appreciated worldwide.  The pioneering research of scholars working in the country’s higher education institutions and academies, such as Dmytro Mendeleyev, Mykola Zhukovsky, and Yeugeny Paton, are part of the universal history of scientific progress.

Russian Academy of Engineering | The Russian Academy of Engineering (RAE) is a public academy of sciences, which unites leading Russian and foreign scientists, engineers, scientific-research organizations, higher educational institutions and enterprises.  Among the full members of the Academy are the persons who enriched the science with outstanding achievements or practically implemented works of high scientific-technical significance in the area of engineering, who made their great personal contribution into organizing one of branches of industry, science, technics and technology.

Institute of Higher Education of NAPS of Ukraine | Institute of Higher Education of NAPS (National Academy of Pedagogical Sciences) of Ukraine - is one of the NAPS Institutes responsible for control, research and development of system of higher education.

Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky | Prof Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky (Russian: Никола́й Его́рович Жуко́вский ; January 17 [O.S. January 5] 1847  – March 17, 1921) was a Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics.  Whereas contemporary scientists scoffed at the idea of human flight, Zhukovsky was the first to undertake the study of airflow.  He is often called the Father of Russian Aviation.

Moscow State University of Civil Engineering | Moscow State University of Civil Engineering or MGSU (Russian: Московский Государственный Строительный Университет - МГСУ) is a higher education institution located in Moscow, Russia.  MGSU is considered to be flagship of technical science, professional education and advanced research of the civil engineering field within the Russian Federation.  The university holds the status of National Research University which is a federal embodiment for universities aimed to be the core of modernization of science and education sector.

National University Odesa Law Academy | National University "Odesa Law Academy" () is a higher educational institution in Odesa.  The University was founded in 1997 on the basis of Law Institute of Odesa I.I. Mechnikov National University.  Today NU "OLA" is the center of legal education, culture and science of Ukraine, leading higher educational institution of jurisprudence.  According to the State Accreditation Commission of Ministry of Education and Science, Youth and Sports of Ukraine, the University trains specialists in the highest - IV - level of accreditation.

Robert Zemsky | Robert Zemsky (b. 1941) is a Professor of Education, the Chair of the Learning Alliance for Higher Education, and the founding director of the Institute for Research on Higher Education at the University of Pennsylvania Graduate School of Education.  he pioneered the use of prospective student market analyses in higher education.
hop1_titles: Institute of Higher Education of NAPS of Ukraine
Moscow State University of Civil Engineering
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- "Dmytro Mendeleyev" — [1]: "Dmytro Mendeleyev"
- "Mykola Zhukovsky" — [1]: "Mykola Zhukovsky"
- "Yeugeny Paton" — [1]: "Yeugeny Paton"
- "Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky" — [4]: "a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics."

Ambiguities:
- "Mykola Zhukovsky" (Passage [1]) vs "Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky" (Passage [4]) — name-form variants.
- "Dmytro Mendeleyev" may conflict with "Dmitri/Dmitry Mendeleev" spelling variants.
- "Yeugeny Paton" may correspond to "Yevgeny/Evgeny Paton" variants.

Bridge / missing fact:
- None essential to answer: Passage [4] explicitly identifies Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky with the required description; only a name-variant mapping (Mykola ↔ Nikolay) is needed and is straightforward.

Retrieval direction:
- Open page "Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky" (verify biography and exact phrasing).
- Search "Mykola Zhukovsky Nikolay Zhukovsky" (verify name-variant equivalence).
- (Optional) Open "Yeugeny Paton" / "Yevgeny Paton" and "Dmytro Mendeleyev" pages for context.
Final conclusion: Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky (matches "Mykola Zhukovsky" in [1]) is the scientist described.
hop2_docs: Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky | Prof Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky (Russian: Никола́й Его́рович Жуко́вский ; January 17 [O.S. January 5] 1847  – March 17, 1921) was a Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer, and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics.  Whereas contemporary scientists scoffed at the idea of human flight, Zhukovsky was the first to undertake the study of airflow.  He is often called the Father of Russian Aviation.

Zhukovsky (film) | Zhukovsky (Russian: Жуковский ) is a 1950 Soviet biopic directed by Vsevolod Pudovkin and Dmitri Vasilyev, based on the life of Russian scientist Nikolai Zhukovsky (1847–1921), founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics.  In 1950 Pudovkin received the Best Director award at the 5th Karlovy Vary International Film Festival for this film.  In 1951 Pudovkin, Shebalin, Golovnya, and Belokurov received the Stalin Prize.

Nikolay Ivanovich Zhukovsky | Nikolay Ivanovich Zhukovsky (Russian: Николай Иванович Жуковский ; (November 2 [O.S. October 21] 1833 in Ufa – May 10 [O.S. April 28] 1895 ) was a Russian revolutionary and narodnik, a follower of Mikhail Bakunin; he was born in Ufa and died in Geneva.
Nikolay Matyukhin | Nikolay Ivanovich Matyukhin (Russian: Николай Иванович Матюхин ; born December 13, 1968 in Zhukovsky) is a Russian race walker.  He is married to the sprinter Tatyana Chebykina.

DeepFlight Challenger | DeepFlight Challenger is a one-person personal submarine deep submergence vehicle with full ocean depth capability.  It is an "aero-submarine" which uses hydrodynamic forces to descend, as the sub has positive buoyancy, utilizing DeepFlight technology from Hawkes Ocean Technologies.  The submarine is currently owned by Virgin Oceanic.

Zhukovsky International Airport | Zhukovsky International Airport (IATA: ZIA, ICAO: UUBW) , also known as Ramenskoye Airport or Zhukovsky Airfield (Russian: Аэропорт Раменское , Аэропорт Жуковский), is Moscow's fourth international airport.  It is located in Moscow Oblast, Russia 40 km southeast of central Moscow, in the town of Zhukovsky, a few kilometers southeast of the old Bykovo Airport.

Access road to Zhukovsky | The Access road to Zhukovsky from M5 highway (also known as the "Access road from M5 highway to LII") is a road running across a distance of 9 km from settlement Imeni Telmana to the Zhukovsky.
response.summary_2: Mykola Zhukovsky corresponds to Nikolay Yegorovich Zhukovsky, who is identified in the passages as a Russian scientist, mathematician and engineer and a founding father of modern aero- and hydrodynamics.
